terms.def: Agreed price for future delivery; set by no-arbitrage: F = S × e^(r−q)T where q is dividend/convenience yield.
Spot price − futures price; converges to zero at expiry. Basis risk = uncertainty in this convergence.
Futures price > spot price; typical for financial futures (storage costs + financing). Forward curve slopes up.
Futures price < spot price; common in commodities with high convenience yield or supply shortages.
P&L from rolling a futures position (selling expiring contract, buying next); positive in backwardation.
Collateral deposited to open a futures position; marked to market daily (variation margin) through the exchange.

eli3: A futures contract is an agreement to buy or sell an asset at a predetermined price on a future date. Unlike options, both parties are obligated to follow through. Futures are standardised, exchange-traded contracts — the exchange guarantees the trade, so counterparty risk is minimal.

Futures exist for equity indices (S&P 500), bonds (10-year Treasury), currencies (EUR/USD), commodities (crude oil, gold, corn), and interest rates. They are heavily used for hedging (protecting against price moves) and speculation (taking leveraged directional bets). A futures contract typically requires only a small initial margin (2-10% of notional) — giving enormous leverage.

Key feature: futures prices incorporate the 'cost of carry' — the interest cost of financing the position minus any dividends or yield earned. The fair value of a futures contract is: F = S × e^{(r-q)T}, where S is spot price, r is risk-free rate, q is dividend yield, and T is time to expiry.

whenWhy.title: Hedging with index futures
whenWhy.body: A portfolio manager holding a $100M equity portfolio wants to reduce market exposure by 50% ahead of an uncertain economic release, without selling the actual stocks (tax implications, transaction costs). Solution: short S&P 500 futures to reduce beta. Number of contracts = (target β - current β) × Portfolio_value / (Futures_price × Contract_multiplier). For S&P 500 E-mini futures (multiplier = $50): if portfolio β = 1.0, S&P 500 at 5,000, portfolio = $100M: contracts to short = (0.5 - 1.0) × $100M / (5,000 × $50) = -200 contracts. After hedging: portfolio is 50% hedged. Restoring exposure is equally fast — cover the short futures. Cost: only bid-ask spread + commission (~$1-2/contract). Advantage over selling stocks: no market impact, no realised gains, instantly reversible.

whenWhy.title: Roll return and futures curve structure
whenWhy.body: Futures contracts expire; to maintain continuous exposure, you must 'roll' — close the expiring contract and open the next one. Roll return = price difference between the near contract and the next contract. Contango: futures price > spot price (far contract more expensive); negative roll return — you sell cheap and buy dear. Backwardation: futures price < spot price (far contract cheaper); positive roll return — you sell dear and buy cheap. Equity futures: typically slight contango (interest cost > dividend yield in low-yield stocks). Commodity futures: can be in either state depending on storage costs and supply/demand. Example: crude oil in contango → long crude futures earns negative roll return → long-term commodity exposure is costly. This explains why commodity ETFs tracking futures underperform spot commodity prices in persistent contango.

whenWhy.title: Basis risk and limitations of futures hedges
whenWhy.body: Basis = spot price - futures price. Basis risk: the hedge is imperfect because the asset you're hedging may not move exactly with the futures index. Example: hedging a small-cap portfolio with S&P 500 futures — if small-caps underperform large-caps, the hedge removes market risk but leaves residual small-cap vs. large-cap risk unhedged. Sources of basis risk: (1) Index mismatch (your portfolio ≠ futures index); (2) Currency mismatch (US futures, foreign portfolio); (3) Term mismatch (futures expire at a different date than your hedge horizon). Managing basis risk: use the most correlated available futures; run a hedge ratio regression to find optimal hedge ratio; accept residual basis risk as the cost of an imperfect but cheap hedge.

deepDiveIntro: Cost-of-carry model: F(t,T) = S(t) × e^{(r - q)(T-t)}, where r = risk-free rate, q = continuous dividend yield (or convenience yield for commodities). For bond futures: F = (S - I) × e^{r(T-t)}, where I is PV of coupons paid before expiry. For currency futures (covered interest parity): F = S × e^{(r_d - r_f)(T-t)}, where r_d and r_f are domestic and foreign risk-free rates. Mark-to-market: futures positions are settled daily; gains/losses credited/debited to margin account daily. Initial margin (IM) ≈ 2-10% of notional; variation margin covers daily P&L. Margin call triggered when account falls below maintenance margin.

deepDive.heading: Futures Pricing and Cash-and-Carry Arbitrage
deepDive.body: If F > S × e^{(r-q)T}: cash-and-carry arbitrage — buy spot, sell futures, earn riskless profit. Steps: (1) Borrow S at rate r; (2) Buy underlying at spot S; (3) Short futures at F; (4) At expiry: deliver underlying, receive F; repay loan S × e^{rT}; earn dividends S × e^{qT}. Net profit: F - S × e^{(r-q)T} > 0. This arbitrage is self-correcting: buying spot raises S; shorting futures lowers F until equality holds. Practical limits: transaction costs, short-selling constraints, margin requirements mean a small band around fair value is arbitrage-free. For equity index futures, arbitrage is done by index arbitrageurs (usually sell-side desks) maintaining tight fair value. [1]

deepDive.heading: Futures vs. Forwards: Key Differences
deepDive.body: Futures and forwards are both agreements to transact at a future date, but differ structurally: (1) Exchange-traded vs. OTC: futures are standardised, exchange-cleared, centralised counterparty; forwards are bilateral OTC contracts with counterparty credit risk; (2) Mark-to-market vs. settlement at expiry: futures settle daily (variation margin), creating cash flows throughout the life; forwards settle once at maturity; (3) Standardisation: futures have fixed expiry dates, standard contract sizes, and delivery specifications; forwards are fully customisable; (4) Liquidity: futures markets are extremely liquid (S&P 500 E-mini: $200B+ daily volume); forward markets are less liquid; (5) Convexity adjustment: for interest rate futures (Eurodollar), the daily settlement creates a convexity bias vs. forwards; futures rate ≠ forward rate (usually lower by a convexity adjustment). In practice: futures are used for hedging liquid index exposure; forwards are used for FX hedging of specific cash flows. [1]

deepDive.heading: Commodity Futures and the Theory of Storage
deepDive.body: Commodity futures pricing is governed by the theory of storage: F = S × e^{(r + u - c)T}, where u = storage cost (% of spot per year), c = convenience yield (benefit of holding physical commodity). Convenience yield: the implicit benefit from physical ownership — ability to meet unexpected demand, avoid supply disruption. High convenience yield → backwardation. Low convenience yield (abundant supply) → contango. Energy markets: crude oil is usually in contango when storage is ample; in backwardation when supply is tight (immediate demand > storage capacity). Gold: minimal convenience yield (gold is not consumed in production); typically in contango (storage cost positive). Agricultural commodities: highly seasonal; convenience yield peaks pre-harvest (tight supply); falls post-harvest. For investors: backwardated commodity futures earn positive roll yield — this has historically contributed 2-4% annual return to commodity index returns (though periods of persistent contango, like 2005-2010 for oil, can reverse this). [2]

takeaways: Futures fair value: F = S × e^{(r-q)T}; cash-and-carry arbitrage keeps this equality tight. [1]
Equity hedge: contracts = (target β - current β) × AUM / (futures price × multiplier). [1]
Contango: negative roll return (buy dear, sell cheap); backwardation: positive roll return. [2]
Daily mark-to-market: variation margin creates cash flows; differs from forwards (single settlement at expiry). [1]
Basis risk: imperfect correlation between hedged asset and futures index leaves residual risk. [1]

edgeCases.q: What happens if you can't deliver the underlying at expiry?
edgeCases.a: Most futures contracts are cash-settled: instead of physical delivery, the holder receives (F_expiry - F_entry) in cash. Examples: S&P 500 E-mini, Eurodollar, VIX futures. Physically delivered contracts (crude oil, agricultural, some Treasury futures) require actual delivery of the underlying — most speculators close positions before expiry to avoid delivery obligations. Delivery specification matters: crude oil futures specify West Texas Intermediate (WTI) at Cushing, Oklahoma — not any crude anywhere. A trader who forgets to close a long crude futures position faces obligation to take delivery of 1,000 barrels at a specific location. In April 2020, WTI futures went briefly negative (-$37/barrel) because storage at Cushing was full and holders faced the impossible cost of taking delivery with no storage available.

edgeCases.q: How do interest rate futures (SOFR futures) work?
edgeCases.a: SOFR (Secured Overnight Financing Rate) futures replaced Eurodollar futures as the primary USD interest rate futures. 3-month SOFR futures: price = 100 - annualised 3-month SOFR rate. If futures price = 95.00, implied rate = 5.00%. Tick: 0.005 = $12.50 per contract (contract size $1M notional). Used for: (1) Hedging floating rate loan exposure; (2) Expressing views on Fed rate path; (3) Constructing term SOFR rates from futures curve. The futures strip (series of quarterly expirations) implies the market's expectation of the Fed funds rate path over 1-3 years. Convexity adjustment: SOFR futures rate ≠ OIS forward rate exactly; a small convexity adjustment arises from daily mark-to-market vs. single settlement at maturity.

sources.label: Hull, J.C. — Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives (10th ed.), Pearson
Gorton, G. & Rouwenhorst, K.G. (2006) — Facts and Fantasies about Commodity Futures, FAJ
